title: Story Time: An Adventure in Tibet
explanation_chunks: On this page, you get to read an exciting part of a story called 'Running on the Roof of the World'. It's a fiction story, which means it's made up, but it feels very real.
The story is about two children, Tash and Sam, who are racing home from school in a place called Tibet. It sounds like a beautiful but wild place with mountains and barley fields.
As you read, try to imagine what it feels like for Tash, who is running super fast and feeling the wind, then suddenly gets a big surprise.
Pay attention to how the writer uses words to make you feel Tash's energy, and then her sudden fear, when she spots something unexpected.
This extract is just the beginning, but it immediately pulls you into their adventure and makes you wonder what will happen next!
examples: The wind 'bites, stinging my nose' – this makes you feel how cold and strong the wind is.
Tash says she 'soar like the golden eagles' – this shows she feels free and fast, like a bird flying high.
Her school bag 'thuds against my back' – this helps you hear the sound and feel the weight of her bag as she runs.
When Sam shouts, 'Soldiers,' Tash's 'stomach drops' – this describes the sudden feeling of fear she experiences.
key_points: Stories use descriptive language to create vivid pictures in your mind.
Characters in fiction often have strong feelings and reactions.
Setting details help you understand where and when a story takes place.
An exciting beginning can make readers want to know more.

vocabulary.definition: A story that is made up and not based on real events.
A small part or section taken from a larger text, like a book.
Covered with small stones and pebbles.
Fields where barley, a type of grain, is grown.
Surrounded or shut in by something.
Causing a sharp, smarting pain, like an insect bite.
To fly or rise high in the air.
Make a soft, muffled sound, like dry leaves or paper moving.
To interrupt the quiet or calm of something.
A type of insect that can release a bad smell when disturbed.
The worst or most complete loss possible.
Makes a sharp, sibilant sound, like a snake.
To identify someone or something you have seen before.
Wanting or being prepared to do something; in this context, hoping something would happen.

explain_again: Imagine you're watching a movie clip! This page gives you a piece of an adventure story about two kids racing home. They're having fun, then suddenly, BAM! Soldiers! It makes you really want to know what happens next.
fun_analogy: Reading this story extract is like getting a tiny sneak peek behind the curtains before a big play – it gives you just enough to get excited and curious!
